content: Ok, what asset should I realocate from the sell of 50% of my gold?

args.thought: User needs allocation advice for ~20% of portfolio (from 50% of their 40% gold position). Key constraints: 6-12 month capital need, so capital preservation is priority #1. Cannot recommend volatile assets for money they'll need soon. Need to think through: (1) How much is truly needed in 6-12 months vs. can be invested longer, (2) Current market yields and opportunities, (3) Risk-adjusted options for different time horizons within that 6-12 month window.

Need to clarify the actual dollar amount and confirm the 6-12 month timeline. For capital needed within 12 months, the investment universe is severely limited: money market funds, T-bills, short-term CDs, and possibly short-duration bond funds. Equities and long-duration bonds are inappropriate due to volatility risk. Current yields: MMFs ~4.5-5.0%, 6-month T-bills ~4.8%, 12-month T-bills ~4.6% (as of early 2026). Need to verify current rates.

results.title: 6 Month Treasury Rate - Real-Time & Historical Yield Trends
highlights:
6 Month Treasury Rate is at 3.68%, compared to 3.66% the previous market day and 4.29% last year. This is higher than the long term average of 2.91%. The 6 Month Treasury Bill Rate is the yield received for investing in a US government issued treasury security that has a maturity of 6 months. The 6 month treasury yield is included on the shorter end of the yield curve. The 6 month treasury yield reached nearly 16% in 1981, as the Fed was raising its benchmark rates in an effort to curb inflation. args.thought: Current yield data (March 2026): 6-month T-bills ~3.63-3.69%, 12-month T-bills ~3.59%, money market funds (VMFXX, VMMXX) likely similar ~3.6-3.7%. For capital needed in 6-12 months, the recommendation is clear: prioritize capital preservation over yield chasing. The 0.10% yield difference between 6mo and 12mo T-bills isn't worth duration risk if money is needed at month 7.

Final recommendation structure: For 6-12 month capital needs, the priority is capital preservation, not yield maximization. Recommend a ladder approach: 50% in 6-month T-bills (~3.63%), 50% in money market fund (VMFXX/VMMXX ~3.6-3.7%) for flexibility. This balances yield with liquidity. Avoid: equities, long-duration bonds, corporate bonds, anything with principal risk.
